title: Ultrafast Energy Absorption in Silicon Controlled by Two-Color Double Pulses
authors: Eiyu S. Gushiken, Mizuki Tani, Hiroki Katow, Kenichi L. Ishikawa
abstract: We theoretically show that energy absorption in crystalline silicon can be controlled by two-color femtosecond double-pulse irradiation, in which two temporally separated pulses with different wavelengths interact sequentially with the system. Using time-dependent density functional theory, we systematically examine the wavelength and intensity dependence of the absorbed energy over peak intensities of $2\times10^{11}$-$10^{13}$ W/cm$^2$ and wavelengths of 515, 1030, and 2060 nm. We find that the mechanism governing energy absorption and the optimal wavelength combination strongly depend on the intensity regime. In the low-intensity regime, multiphoton interband absorption is dominant, and energy absorption is enhanced for pulse pairs composed of shorter wavelengths. In contrast, in the high-intensity regime, the contributions of tunneling ionization and intraband acceleration become significant, leading to enhanced absorption for longer-wavelength combinations. In the intermediate-intensity regime, a pronounced enhancement is observed when a short-wavelength pulse precedes a long-wavelength pulse. Our analysis reveals that the nonequilibrium electronic state prepared by the first pulse modifies the excitation process induced by the second pulse, thereby enhancing the absorbed energy through an increased energy gain per excited electron. In this regime, the energy absorption is governed not only by the number of excited carriers but also by the energy gain per excited electron, which can be strongly modified by the pulse sequence. These results indicate that ultrafast energy transfer in semiconductors is tunable by appropriately designing the wavelength and intensity combination of the two pulses, and provide microscopic insight into two-color strong-field excitation.
summary: This paper demonstrates that energy deposition in silicon can be precisely tuned using two-color laser pulses. By adjusting the wavelength and the order of the pulses, one can switch between mechanisms like multiphoton absorption and intraband acceleration. Specifically, a short-wavelength pulse followed by a long-wavelength pulse optimizes energy absorption by increasing the energy gain of each excited electron. This provides a microscopic blueprint for controlling ultrafast energy transfer in semiconductors.
main_problem: The study investigates how the wavelength, intensity, and temporal sequence of two-color femtosecond double-pulses can be used to control energy absorption and electron dynamics in crystalline silicon.
main_result: The researchers found that energy absorption is maximized in an intermediate intensity regime when a short-wavelength pulse precedes a long-wavelength pulse, driven by increased energy gain per electron through intraband acceleration.
method: The study uses Time-Dependent Density Functional Theory (TDDFT) implemented via the SALMON code, employing the Kohn-Sham equations in the velocity gauge.
model_or_system: The physical system is crystalline (bulk) silicon with a diamond structure. The model utilizes a periodic 8-atom unit cell with norm-conserving pseudopotals and a modified Becke-Johnson exchange-correlation potential.
key_observables: Total absorbed energy, induced current density, number of excited electrons, mean absorbed energy per electron, and Differential Density of States (DDOS).
important_parameters: Wavelengths of 515, 1030, and 2060 nm; peak intensities from 2e11 to 1e13 W/cm2; pulse delay times; and pulse sequence order.
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the initial coherent transient regime (up to 65 fs) and does not account for long-term electron-phonon energy transfer or thermal equilibrium.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows temporal profiles of the electric field and current density; Figure 7 shows absorbed energy and electron number vs. delay time; Figure 16 and 17 show the evolution of the Differential Density of States and electron populations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ultrafast energy control, describes the TDDFT methodology and silicon model, presents results across low, intermediate, and high intensity regimes, analyzes the microscopic mechanisms of carrier excitation and acceleration, and concludes with implications for tunable energy transfer.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Temporal profile of the electric field E(t) (green solid, left axis) and induced current density J(t) (purple dashed, right axis) for a double-pulse 515 + 2060 nm, I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2, Tdelay = 35 fs.

FIG. 3. (a) Temporal profile of the calculated number of excited carriers for each wavelength combination with I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2 and Tdelay = 35 fs. (b) Total number of excited carriers for each wavelength combination.
FIG. 2. (a) Temporal profile of the absorbed energy for each wavelength combination with I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2

Figure 3 shows the time evolution [panel (a)] and fi- nal values [panel (b)] of the number of excited electrons under each wavelength combination. When the wave- length is shorter, i.e., the photon energy is higher, the first pulse excites more electrons due to more efficient interband transitions (See around 0–30 fs in panel (a)). However, the final number of excited electrons after the second pulse does not strongly depend on the wavelength combination, compared with the absorbed energy shown in Fig. 2(a). These results suggest that the increased energy deposition observed for certain double-pulse con- figurations arises primarily from a higher energy gain per excited carrier, rather than...
FIG. 4. Mean absorbed energy per excited electron for each wavelength combination with I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2

FIG. 5. DDOS at t = 35 and 65 fs, corresponding to the times immediately after the first and second laser pulses, re- spectively, for I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2. Panels (a) and (b) show the results for the 515+2060 nm and 2060+515 nm, respectively.
FIG. 6. Number of electrons excited to states within and be- yond the projection energy range ϵmax = 15 eV after double- pulse irradiation at I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2.

FIG. 7. Total absorbed energy and number of excited elec- trons as a function of the delay time Tdelay in the 515 + 2060 nm configuration. The intensity is fixed at I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2. A positive delay means that the 515 nm pulse precedes the 2060 nm pulse.

FIG. 8. Relative phase dependence of the absorbed energy at 515 + 2060 nm, Tdelay = 35 fs, and I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2. ϕ1 and ϕ2 denote the carrier–envelope phases of the 515 nm and 2060 nm pulses, respectively.

Figure 7 shows the total absorbed energy and the num- ber of excited electrons as functions of the delay time Tdelay at I1 = I2 = 3.5 × 1012 W/cm2. A positive de- lay means that the 515 nm pulse precedes the 2060 nm pulse. When the pulses overlap (−20 ≲Tdelay ≲20), the energy absorption is increased, as we have previously reported [40]. In contrast, when the pulses are well sepa- rated, both the absorbed energy and the excited electron number are virtually independent of the delay time.
